perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non-contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the law of England and Wales.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted according to the definitions outlined in Section 1.2. For purposes of interpreting the phrase 'Confidential Information' only, this Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non-contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the law of England and Wales.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the governing law clause to be specifically tied to a definition, instead of applying to the whole document, creates ambiguity and potential unenforceability. Governing law clauses should apply to the entire contract, not just a specific section.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English law requires clarity and certainty in contract terms, especially regarding governing law, as per principles of contract interpretation established in common law.
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of Contract Interpretation in English Law
perturbation.law_explanation: By limiting the application of the governing law clause to the definition of 'Confidential Information', the modification introduces uncertainty as to which law governs other aspects of the contract, potentially violating the principle that contracts should be interpreted to give effect to the intentions of the parties as a whole and with certainty. It also deviates from standard contract drafting practices where the governing law applies to all aspects of the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the governing law clause to only the definition of 'Confidential Information,' which contradicts the standard practice of applying the governing law to the entire contract. This introduces uncertainty about which law applies to other aspects of the contract, potentially leading to disputes and unenforceability as the clause no longer clearly applies to the whole agreement, which is a structural flaw. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that the modification "introduces uncertainty as to which law governs other aspects of the contract" and "deviates from standard contract drafting practices where the governing law applies to all aspects of the agreement." This falls under the definition of 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' as the placement of the governing law clause affects the clarity and enforceability of the contract.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for any breach of the terms of this Agreement. Accordingly, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without proof of special damages, to an injunction or other equitable remedy for any threatened or actual breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that for the purposes of Section 2.1 only, damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for any breach of the terms of this Agreement. Accordingly, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without proof of special damages, to an injunction or other equitable remedy for any threatened or actual breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the availability of injunctive relief to only one section of the contract introduces a structural flaw. Injunctive relief should be available for all breaches of confidentiality, not just breaches of one specific section. This reduces the overall enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of equity in English contract law regarding remedies for breach of contract.
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of Equitable Remedies in English Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Equity dictates that remedies should be available when damages are inadequate to compensate for a breach. By restricting the availability of injunctive relief to Section 2.1 only, the modification implies that damages are adequate for breaches of other sections, undermining the purpose of seeking equitable remedies when monetary compensation is insufficient. This could be seen as an unreasonable limitation on remedies, potentially unenforceable under English law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Home - Courts and Tribunals Judiciary Skip to main content Latest judgments June 23, 2025 Yusef Ibrahim -v- Rex June 23, 2025 CCN -v- Junaid Dar (anonymity order) June 23, 2025 YLG and YLH -v- Hampshire County Council (anonymity order) June 20, 2025 R -v- Wasif Hussain and Nabela Tabassum See more judgments Latest news June 18, 2025 Chapter 19: Trans people and the Coroner’s Court This is a resource for coroners to help them locate key principles, practical information and precedents when dealing with inquests June 9, 2025 Practice Note: Commercial Court and London Circuit Commercial Court See this guidance from the President of the King’s Bench Division, and Judges in Charge of the Commercial Court and London Circuit Commercial Court June 9, 2025 In memoriam: The Right Honourable Lord Justice William Davis The Lady Chief Justice pays tribute to Lord Justice William Davis June 6, 2025 Fee-paid Judge of the First-tier Tribunal (Property Chamber) Pre-Application Seminar 2025 Find out about this event on Tuesday 24 June 2025, including how to register See more news Find out more Business and Property Courts Specialist courts which decide business, commercial, property and other chancery disputes and technology and construction disputes, both domestic and international. Court of Appeal The Court of Appeal deals exclusively with people challenging the decisions made by other courts or tribunals. It has two divisions, Criminal and Civil (the latter also hears family appeals). High Court The High Court hears the most serious administrative, civil and family cases. It has three divisions – King’s Bench, Family and Chancery. Tribunals The Tribunals are specialist judicial bodies whose judges and members hear a wide range of cases, such as tax, employment, and immigration and asylum. The Upper Tribunal and Employment Appeal Tribunal hear appeals challenging some tribunal decisions. See more courts and tribunals Magistrates’ courts Magistrates, or Justices of the Peace as they are also known, are trained, volunteer members of the local community who make decisions in criminal and family cases in Magistrates’ courts and the Family Court. The most complex cases in Magistrates’ courts are heard by District Judges who are legally qualified specialists. Coroners’ courts Coroners investigate certain deaths which are deemed to be unnatural, violent, or where the cause is unknown. Popular links Family Justice Council Civil Justice Council Appointments and retirements Court of Appeal live hearings
perturbation.justification: The original contract ensures injunctive relief for any breach, while the modified text restricts it to Section 2.1 only. This creates a structural flaw by limiting the availability of a crucial remedy, potentially making it inadequate for breaches outside Section 2.1. The LLM explanation supports this by highlighting that equity dictates remedies should be available when damages are inadequate, and restricting injunctive relief undermines this principle. The scraped snippets do not directly apply to this issue, but they illustrate the importance of the courts in resolving disputes, indicating the potential for this restriction to be challenged.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement contains the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior agreements and understandings, both written and oral, between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of determining the meaning of the word 'Information', this Agreement contains the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior agreements and understandings, both written and oral, between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: By restricting the 'entire agreement' clause to only the definition of 'Information', prior negotiations or side agreements related to other aspects of the contract may be argued to be still valid, directly contradicting the purpose of the entire agreement clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of contract interpretation and the parol evidence rule under English law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The parol evidence rule generally prevents parties from introducing evidence of prior or contemporaneous agreements to contradict the terms of a written contract intended to be the complete and final expression of their agreement. By limiting the 'entire agreement' clause, the modification opens the door for such extrinsic evidence to be admissible, potentially undermining the written terms and violating the principles of contract interpretation that seek to give effect to the parties' intentions as expressed in the final written agreement.

perturbation.justification: The modification in the contract restricts the 'entire agreement' clause to only the definition of 'Information.' According to the provided information, this allows prior negotiations or side agreements related to other aspects of the contract to potentially remain valid, directly opposing the entire agreement clause's intended purpose. The Law Explanation also indicates that this modification opens the door for extrinsic evidence to be admissible, potentially undermining the written terms. While the scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation strongly support the conclusion that this change creates a legal contradiction because it weakens the enforceability of the contract's written terms by allowing external agreements to be considered.

perturbation.original_text: No failure or delay by a Party in exercising any right or remedy under this Agreement shall operate as a waiver thereof, nor shall any single or partial exercise of any right or remedy preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any other right or remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: In regard to Section 1.1, No failure or delay by a Party in exercising any right or remedy under this Agreement shall operate as a waiver thereof, nor shall any single or partial exercise of any right or remedy preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any other right or remedy.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly tying the 'no waiver' clause to a specific section (Section 1.1), it implies that a failure or delay in exercising rights under *other* sections of the agreement *could* be construed as a waiver. The original clause is meant to prevent accidental waivers across the entire agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of contract interpretation regarding waiver of rights under English law.
perturbation.law_explanation: English law recognizes that a party may waive a right under a contract, but such waiver must be clear and unequivocal. The original clause aims to prevent unintended waivers. By limiting the no-waiver provision to Section 1.1, the modification suggests that inaction or delay in enforcing rights under other sections could be interpreted as a waiver, making the enforceability of those sections uncertain and potentially violating the principle that waivers should not be lightly inferred.

perturbation.justification: The original text provides a blanket 'no waiver' provision for the entire agreement. The changed text limits this provision to Section 1.1. This creates a structural flaw because it implies that the 'no waiver' clause does not apply to other sections, potentially undermining the enforceability of rights and remedies in those sections. While the provided snippets do not explicitly define 'waiver,' the Perturbation and Law Explanations both clarify how the change could lead to implied waivers in sections outside of 1.1, contradicting the intent of the original clause to prevent unintended waivers across the entire agreement. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition because the clause is placed in a way that affects its clarity and enforceability regarding the entire contract.
